title: Event Sourcing + CQRS
summary: Event sourcing as the system of record, CQRS read/write separation, projections, and event store design.

sections.content:
## Traditional State vs Event Sourcing

**Traditional**: Store current state — overwrite on each update.
```sql
UPDATE account SET balance=500 WHERE id=1  -- old balance gone forever
```

**Event Sourcing**: Store the sequence of events — derive current state by replaying.
```
Event 1: AccountOpened { balance: 1000 }
Event 2: MoneyWithdrawn { amount: 200 }
Event 3: MoneyDeposited { amount: 300 }
Event 4: MoneyWithdrawn { amount: 600 }

Current balance = 1000 - 200 + 300 - 600 = 500
```

The **event log is the source of truth**, not a derived table.

sections.title: Event Sourcing — State from Events
sections.content:
graph LR
    E1[AccountOpened\n+1000] --> E2[Withdrawn\n-200]
    E2 --> E3[Deposited\n+300]
    E3 --> E4[Withdrawn\n-600]
    E4 --> State[Current State\nbalance=500]
    E1 & E2 & E3 & E4 --> Log[Immutable\nEvent Store]
    style Log fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style State fill:#059669,color:#fff

## Benefits of Event Sourcing

- **Complete audit log** — every change is recorded with who, when, why
- **Time travel** — replay events to see state at any point in time
- **Event replay** — rebuild projections from scratch
- **Debugging** — reproduce bugs by replaying exact event sequence
- **Integration** — other systems consume events (outbox for free)
- **Business value** — events are meaningful business facts

## Challenges

- **Schema evolution** — events are immutable; must handle old event versions (upcasting)
- **Eventual consistency** — projections may lag behind
- **Querying** — can't easily query event-sourced data directly (need projections)
- **Snapshot optimization** — replaying 10M events to get current state is slow

sections.title: CQRS + Event Sourcing Architecture
sections.content:
graph LR
    C[Client]
    C -->|Command| CH[Command Handler]
    CH -->|validate + apply| ES[Event Store\nImmutable log]
    ES -->|publish| EP[Event Processor]
    EP -->|update| RM1[Read Model 1\nOrder Summary Table]
    EP -->|update| RM2[Read Model 2\nSearch Index]
    EP -->|update| RM3[Read Model 3\nAnalytics]
    C -->|Query| QH[Query Handler]
    QH --> RM1
    style ES fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style RM1 fill:#059669,color:#fff
    style RM2 fill:#059669,color:#fff
    style RM3 fill:#059669,color:#fff

sections.title: Event Sourcing is not for every domain
sections.content: Event sourcing adds significant complexity. It shines for: financial systems (audit trail required), domains where history matters (order lifecycle), collaboration tools, and GDPR compliance (events allow selective deletion). For simple CRUD domains, traditional state storage is usually better.

sections.question: What is a projection in Event Sourcing?
options: A snapshot of aggregate state at a specific point in time
A read model built by processing the event stream to serve specific query needs
A future event scheduled for execution
A validation rule applied to incoming commands
sections.explanation: A projection is a read model built by subscribing to the event stream and building a query-optimized view. For example, an 'OrderSummary' projection listens for OrderPlaced, OrderShipped, OrderCancelled events and maintains a denormalized table optimized for listing orders. Multiple projections can serve different query needs from the same event stream.
